Motion For Order Compelling Complete Response Of Jay Chen, Person Most Qualified For Defendant Beyond Group, Llc., To Deposition Question; Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Beyond Group, Llc Attorneys Bledsoe Diestel Treppa Crane, Llp
Off Calendar Nov-27-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS CHRISTIAN PINEIRO, VADIM ANANYEV, ARASH GHANADAN'S Motion For Order Compelling Complete Response Of Jay Chen, Person Most Qualified For Defendant Beyond Group, Llc., To Deposition Question; Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Beyond Group, Llc Attorneys Bledsoe Diestel Treppa Crane, Llp

DEFENDANTS CHRISTIAN PINEIRO, VADIM ANANYEV, ARASH GHANADAN'S motion to compel is GRANTED in part, but the parties' cross-requests for sanctions are DENIED.

A communication between two agents of an organization that conveys a privileged communication by an attorney for the organization, or that is made for the purpose of collecting information to enable the attorney to render legal services to the organization, is privileged from discovery, even if the attorney is not a party to the communication. Cal. Evid. Code 952 ("As used in this article, 'confidential communication between client and lawyer' means information transmitted between a client and his or her lawyer in the course of that relationship and in confidence by a means which, so far as the client is aware, discloses the information to no third persons other than those who are present to further the interest of the client in the consultation or those to whom disclosure is reasonably necessary for the transmission of the information or the accomplishment of the purpose for which the lawyer is consulted, and includes a legal opinion formed and the advice given by the lawyer in the course of that relationship." (emphasis supplied); Zurich American Ins. Co. v. Superior Court, 155 Cal. App. 4th 1485 (2007).

Here, Jay Chen, who was designated as the person most qualified to testify on behalf of defendant Beyond Group, LLC, was instructed at deposition not to disclose the content of a conversation he had with Claudia Yu, identified as an agent of defendant, approximately a year after the shooting incident that gave rise to the lawsuit. Although counsel for defendant represented that the conversation involved "[d]iscussing responses to discovery in connection with the request from an attorney," Chen Depo., 54:6-7, counsel was not testifying under oath, and no foundation was laid by the witness himself to support invocation of the privilege. Indeed, when moving defendants counsel asked the witness what was the purpose of the conversation, counsel for defendant objected and instructed the witness not to answer the question. Id. at 54:12-24

Moving Defendants' motion to compel therefore is GRANTED to the extent that (1) the witness must answer questions reasonably designed to elicit the necessary foundation for assertion of the privilege and (2) the witness must answer questions about non-privileged parts of the conversation. To the extent that the foundation is established, assertion of the privilege must be respected. To the extent that the foundation is not established, or that only part of the conversation fell within the privilege, the witness must disclose the non-privileged part of the conversation.

Because the deposition record does not show a lack of substantial justification in the positions taken by either party to this dispute, the parties' cross-requests for sanctions are DENIED.

Order is signed.

Judge Pro Tem: Jeff Wohl; Clerk: William Trupek; Not reported. (302/JPT)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-13-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The Court confirmed its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT BEYOND GROUP LLC'S demurrer to the first, second and fourth causes of action in the complaint is sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to all three claims. None of the three claims sufficiently allege facts to establish a legal duty by Beyond Group to take any affirmative acts to control or prevent criminal conduct of its invitees. (Ann M. v. Pacific Plaza Shopping Center (1993) 6 Cal. 4th 666). On page 7 of his opposition brief plaintiff identifies "general categories of allegations" that he says he can allege in an effort to plead cognizable claims. Because it is possible that depending on what he actually alleges that plaintiff may be able to state claims within the parameters of Ann M. and subsequent cases, plaintiff is given the opportunity to do so. Order signed.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)
